canonicalRequest:
POST
https://api.openai.com/v1/responses
authorization: Bearer <SECRET>
content-type: application/json

{"input":[{"content":"You are an assistant embedded in a terminal app.

Tools:
    * Terminal: execute_command, search_command_history, get_command_history, create_file
    * (Others may be present.)

When to use tools:

1. If the user refers to terminal activity or asks to perform actions:
   - Commands to run: "run", "execute", "install", "update", "open [file]", "save", "create"
   - History queries: "commands I ran", "terminal history" 
   - Action verbs imply doing, not explaining: use execute_command
   - For searching history: use search_command_history
   - For retrieving history: use get_command_history
   
2. If the user asks general questions unrelated to terminal state, answer directly without tools.

Important: 
- Action words (install, run, execute, update, create, open, save) indicate the user wants you to DO something, not explain how.
- Never refuse to act because a tool might be unnecessary. Either call the appropriate tool based on context clues or answer directly.

After gathering evidence via tools, synthesize a clear answer. Be concise and helpful. If a zip file is provided (this is rare), you should extract it and analyze the contents in the context of the accompanying messages.","role":"system","type":"message"},{"content":"Use execute_command to run any command. The output does not matter.","role":"user","type":"message"}],"max_output_tokens":126707,"model":"gpt-5.5","stream":true,"tool_choice":"auto","tools":[{"description":"Runs a shell command and returns its output.","name":"execute_command","parameters":{"additionalProperties":false,"properties":{"command":{"description":"The command to run","type":"string"}},"required":["command"],"type":"object"},"strict":true,"type":"function"},{"description":"Ask the user to enable orchestration mode for this chat. Use this whenever the task requires interacting with the user's iTerm2 sessions and you don't currently have the tools to do it: sending text or keystrokes, running shell commands, reading on-screen output, controlling a running terminal program, watching a session for changes, or coordinating across multiple sessions (Code Review, Diff, Chat, etc.). This applies to single-session tasks as well as multi-session ones; if the user asks you to do something in their terminal and you have no way to act on a session, this is the tool to call. The user is prompted with a description of what orchestration grants and chooses to approve or decline. On approval, this chat switches to the orchestration tool surface (send_text, get_screen_contents, get_state, register_watch, etc.) starting with the next turn; the linked terminal or browser session, if any, is detached. Returns a string describing the outcome.","name":"request_orchestration_enable","parameters":{"additionalProperties":false,"properties":{},"type":"object"},"strict":true,"type":"function"},{"container":{"file_ids":[],"type":"auto"},"type":"code_interpreter"}]}
